input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based incubator** designed for precise temperature regulation within an insulated styrofoam container. Its operational domain is **controlled environmental conditioning**, specifically maintaining a target temperature regardless of internal content variations.

  **Key Components:**
  - **Insulated Container:** A styrofoam box providing thermal isolation to minimize external temperature interference.
  - **Heatbed:** A heating element integrated within the container to raise internal temperatures.
  - **Fan:** A mechanical component distributing heated air uniformly across the interior.
  - **Temperature Sensors:**
    - **Two internal sensors** (plural) for real-time monitoring of the core temperature inside the container.
    - **One external sensor** to measure ambient temperature outside the styrofoam enclosure.
  - **Central Controller:** A **Raspberry Pi** unit managing sensor inputs, actuator commands, and regulatory logic.

  **Technical Suitability for Digital Twinning:**
  The system’s **real-time sensor feedback**, **adaptive control loop**, and **modular hardware** (insulated container, heatbed, fan) enable dynamic modeling and predictive capabilities. Its **CPS nature** (interconnected physical and cyber components) aligns well with DT frameworks requiring synchronized data streams and model calibration for accurate behavior prediction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin (DT) for this incubator system provides the following **core services**, grounded in the provided documents:
  - **Data Collection & Calibration**: The DT integrates **sensor data** (internal/external temperatures) into **model calibration algorithms** (e.g., optimization functions from the Python SciPy package) to update cyber-physical models dynamically. This enables accurate parameter estimation for the heatbed, fan, and thermal environment.
  - **Monitoring & Safety Validation**: The DT employs **real-time monitoring** of temperature deviations from target thresholds, leveraging the PT’s sensors to detect anomalies (e.g., sensor failures or extreme deviations). Violations trigger alerts or automated adjustments via the controller.
  - **Predictive Control**: While not explicitly detailed, the DT’s structure implies **predictive capabilities** for forecasting temperature trends under varying internal loads (e.g., thermal inertia of contents) or external conditions (e.g., ambient temperature fluctuations). This is enabled by the DT’s integration of **simulation models** (e.g., differential equations for dynamic behavior) and calibration data.
  - **Reconfigurability & Robustness**: The DT supports **adaptive control strategies** (e.g., reconfiguring heat distribution or fan speed) to mitigate environmental changes, enhancing the PT’s robustness. This aligns with the incubator’s goal of maintaining temperature stability regardless of internal content variations.
  **Target Users**: The DT primarily serves **engineers, researchers, and operators** involved in:
  - **Maintenance planning** (e.g., predictive maintenance of sensors via DT-driven analytics).
  - **Experimental validation** (e.g., simulating temperature profiles for research purposes).
  - **Operational oversight** (e.g., visualizing real-time DT status and alerts).
  The DT’s value lies in its ability to **bridge the gap between physical and cyber systems**, enabling proactive decision-making and reducing reliance on manual adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation leverages the following **hardware, software, and enabling technologies**:
  - **Hardware**:
    - **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B**: Acts as the central controller for real-time sensor data processing and actuation.
    - **Sensors**: Two internal and one external temperature sensors (details on specific models not provided in documents).
  - **Software and Development Tools**:
    - **Calibration Algorithms**: Implemented using **Python’s SciPy library** for optimization functions (e.g., nonlinear least squares fitting).
    - **Communication Protocol**: **RabbitMQ server** facilitates real-time data exchange between sensors, models, and controllers.
    - **Modeling Framework**: Differential equation-based models (e.g., ODE solvers) represent the Physical Twin’s dynamics, though no finite-element analysis or advanced simulation engines (e.g., COMSOL, ANSYS) are mentioned.
    - **Public Repository**: The **GitHub repository** ([INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) provides up-to-date hardware/software specifications, enabling reproducibility and extensibility.
  - **Enablers (Inferred from Context)**:
    - **Runtime Model Management**: Dynamic updates to models via calibration loops ensure alignment with real-world conditions.
    - **Algorithmic Enablers**: State estimators, fault detectors, and supervisory controllers (implemented via Python/Raspberry Pi) enable autonomous decision-making.
  No explicit mention of visualization tools (e.g., Matplotlib, Plotly), databases (e.g., SQLite, InfluxDB), or cloud platforms is documented.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The Digital Twin (DT) for the incubator system operates with **real-time synchronization** between the physical twin (PT) and digital twin components. The system relies on **periodic data acquisition** from sensors (two internal and one external temperature sensors) via the Raspberry Pi controller, with no explicit frequency specified in the documents. However, the physical dynamics are governed by measurable time constants:
- **Heating element response**: The heatbed takes **under 2 seconds** to adjust temperature changes.
- **Air circulation**: The fan distributes heat within the styrofoam box in approximately **2 seconds**.
The calibration process (Section 3) involves offline optimization on historical data trajectories, implying a **non-real-time** adjustment phase. The DT does not explicitly mention latency requirements or high-resolution temporal granularity, but the control loop operates in **discrete time steps** tied to sensor sampling (implied to be low-frequency, e.g., minutes to hours, based on the offline calibration context). No explicit synchronization protocols (e.g., time synchronization standards like NTP) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The incubator Digital Twin operates as a **single centralized instance** for the entire physical twin system, integrating all hardware components (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, sensors) into one cohesive DT architecture. There is **no mention of decentralized or multiple DT instances**, nor are coordination mechanisms (e.g., message brokers, middleware) between twins described. The scope of responsibility for this DT is **fully autonomous** for temperature regulation and safety monitoring, with no external DT dependencies or hierarchical delegation specified. The DT’s structure aligns with a **unified cyber-physical system (CPS) model**, where the PT and DT share a single orchestration layer (e.g., Raspberry Pi controller).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin constellation for the incubator follows a **closed-loop CPS architecture**, where the DT’s core components include:
  1. **Data Layer**: Real-time sensor data (temperature readings) from the PT (two internal + one external sensors) streamed via the Raspberry Pi.
  2. **Model Layer**: Two calibrated models (not specified further) representing the PT’s thermal dynamics and control system, updated via **calibration algorithms** (e.g., parameter estimation) to match PT behavior.
  3. **Algorithms Layer**: Supervisory controllers, fault detectors, and state estimators (implied by the DT’s role in **safety and optimality checks**) that interact with the PT’s physical actuators (e.g., heatbed/fan adjustments).
  The orchestration pattern is **event-driven**, where model violations (e.g., temperature deviations) trigger human or algorithmic interventions. The DT’s integration with the PT is **direct**, leveraging the Raspberry Pi as the central coordinator, with no explicit mention of middleware (e.g., MQTT, ROS) or distributed coordination protocols. Resource management is implicit but tied to the PT’s hardware constraints (e.g., styrofoam insulation limits thermal dispersion).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) for the thermal incubator system integrates two simulation models designed to predict temperature dynamics:
1. **Two-parameter model**: A simplified behavioral model with two free parameters, used for initial analysis but lacking fidelity for complex thermal behavior.
2. **Four-parameter model**: A more sophisticated behavioral model with four free parameters, specifically optimized to accurately capture the thermal inertia of the heatbed, enabling precise predictions of temperature trajectories over time.

**Data Components**:
- **Data Sources**: Temperature measurements are collected via **DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometers**, deployed to monitor the incubator’s internal environment with an accuracy of **0.5 °C**.
- **Data Management**: Data is transmitted via **1-Wire bus** to support multi-sensor connectivity, ensuring real-time synchronization with the DT’s simulation models.
- **Model-Data Relationships**: The DT employs **calibration algorithms** to iteratively update the four-parameter model’s parameters using empirical data trajectories (experimental runs) to minimize discrepancies between simulated and real-world behavior.

The DT’s data and models are tightly coupled: calibration algorithms dynamically refine the model’s parameters based on sensor-derived data, while the validated model informs safety and optimization monitors for the physical twin (PT).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The physical twin of the incubator system includes:
  - **Heatbed**: A thermally insulated component responsible for maintaining controlled temperature within the incubator.
  - **Fan**: A mechanical actuator circulating air to regulate thermal distribution, operating at predefined voltages (not explicitly quantified in the documents).
  - **Controller**: A **bang-bang controller** (comparable to a discrete on/off regulation scheme) managing actuator behavior, ensuring safe and efficient thermal control. The controller incorporates a **low-level driver with a safe protocol** for hardware interaction.

  **Control Capabilities**:
  - The system does not explicitly describe dynamic adjustments (e.g., PID-based regulation) but confirms the controller’s role in enforcing **safety constraints** via embedded protocols.
  - Actuation is constrained by the physical environment (e.g., thermal inertia of the heatbed) and the controller’s discrete control logic.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The sensing infrastructure comprises:
1. **Sensor Type**: **DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometers**, deployed to measure temperature with an accuracy of **0.5 °C** and a precision sufficient for thermal monitoring.
2. **Spatial Distribution**: Sensors are strategically placed within the incubator to capture temperature gradients across the heatbed and surrounding environment.
3. **Sampling Frequency**: Not explicitly quantified in the documents, but implied to be high-frequency (e.g., real-time or near-real-time) to align with the DT’s dynamic simulation requirements.
4. **Data Transmission Rate**: Transmitted via **1-Wire bus**, enabling low-latency communication between sensors and the DT’s data processing layer.
5. **Data Collection Mechanism**: Sensors collect raw temperature data, which is then processed by the DT’s calibration algorithms to update the behavioral models and inform control decisions.
   - **Transmission Protocol**: Explicitly documented as **1-Wire bus**, not CAN or Ethernet, as per the case study’s hardware setup.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
**Model Fidelity Requirements**:
- The DT prioritizes **thermal inertia modeling** via the **4-parameter model** (Document 2), which outperforms the 2-parameter model in predicting heatbed dynamics.
- **Validation Methods**:
  - **Experimental Validation**: The incubator case study (Document 2) demonstrates empirical testing of the DT’s predictive accuracy against real-world data.
  - **Verification**: Implicitly involves comparing simulation outputs (e.g., temperature profiles) with sensor-measured data to validate model correctness.
- **Uncertainty Sources**:
  - **Model Parameters**: Free parameters (2 or 4) introduce uncertainty; calibration algorithms (Document 1) mitigate this by iteratively refining estimates.
  - **Sensor Noise**: Not explicitly addressed, but assumed to be minimized given the DT’s reliance on high-fidelity temperature measurements.
- **Error Detection/Correction**:
  - **Calibration Algorithms**: Continuously update models using sensor data to correct deviations between PT and DT behavior (Document 1).
  - **Monitoring Systems**: Implied to flag discrepancies (e.g., temperature mismatches) triggering human or algorithmic interventions (Document 1).
- **Quality Assurance**:
  - **Open-Source Accessibility**: Enables community validation and iterative improvement of the DT’s models/data (Document 2).
  - **Hardware-Software Integration**: Ensures consistency between physical sensors and digital representations via closed-loop calibration.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the incubator Digital Twin involves data flows from the physical twin (PT) to the digital twin (DT) via the following mechanisms:

1. **Data Sources and Sensors**:
   - Two internal temperature sensors (DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometers) monitor the temperature inside the styrofoam box.
   - One external temperature sensor monitors the ambient temperature outside the box.
   - Data is transmitted over a **1-Wire bus** for sensor communication.

2. **Data Transmission**:
   - Data is collected in **Celsius (°C)** units (as implied by sensor ranges and operational context, though model calculations may use Kelvin for calculations).
   - **RabbitMQ middleware** acts as the message broker for real-time data transmission between sensors and the DT system.

3. **Data Preprocessing and Quality Assurance**:
   - Calibration of sensor models is performed using the **Python SciPy package** for parameter estimation (not directly handled by the low-level driver).
   - No explicit mention of noise filtering, but the DT likely includes algorithms (e.g., state estimators) to refine raw sensor data.

4. **Event Triggers and Integration**:
   - Data is transmitted continuously or triggered by temperature thresholds (implied by the control loop).
   - Data integration occurs in the **Raspberry Pi** (central node) via middleware (RabbitMQ) to ensure real-time synchronization with the DT models.

No explicit mention of communication protocols (e.g., TCP/IP, MQTT) or transmission frequencies (e.g., Hz, ms) beyond the 1-Wire bus and RabbitMQ infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the incubator Digital Twin involves control flows from the DT to the physical twin (PT) via the following mechanisms:

1. **Control Algorithm**:
   - The control system is **comparable to a bang-bang controller** (implied by the lack of explicit PID tuning or continuous adjustments in the documents).
   - Commands are transmitted through the **low-level driver** with a **safe protocol** to ensure reliable execution.

2. **Command Types and Formats**:
   - Commands are likely **binary or structured** (e.g., ON/OFF for heating/fan control) but not explicitly detailed in the documents.
   - No explicit validation or authentication mechanisms are mentioned beyond the safe protocol in the driver.

3. **Feedback Systems**:
   - The DT uses **real-time sensor data** (from the physical twin) to validate control actions and adjust parameters dynamically.
   - No explicit conflict resolution mechanisms are described, but the DT may employ supervisory controllers (implied by the broader DT framework).

4. **Execution Confirmation**:
   - The Raspberry Pi acts as the central node to execute commands, but confirmation of command execution is not explicitly documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **digital twin’s technical connection** relies on:
1. **1-Wire bus** for sensor data exchange (physical-to-virtual).
2. **RabbitMQ server** as a middleware for real-time message routing, enabling event-driven communication between the DT’s models, algorithms, and physical components.
3. **Cyber-Physical System (CPS) architecture** with a **Raspberry Pi** as the central node, interfacing with the PT’s hardware (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan) via the 1-Wire bus.

No explicit network topology or architecture is detailed, but the system’s modular design suggests a **star topology** centered on the Raspberry Pi. Security protocols are implied by the **safe protocol** in the low-level driver, though no explicit encryption (e.g., TLS) or firewall mechanisms are mentioned. The **Incubator case study repository** ([GitHub](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) may provide additional details on latency/bandwidth requirements, but the documents do not specify quantitative thresholds (e.g., <10ms latency).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for the incubator Digital Twin includes:

1. **Hosting Platform**:
   - The DT runs on the **Raspberry Pi** (on-premise, edge computing) with no mention of cloud or hybrid deployment.

2. **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
   - The DT employs **Python and the SciPy package** for model calibration and algorithm execution (e.g., state estimators, optimisers).
   - No explicit storage requirements are detailed, but the system likely stores calibrated models and historical data on the Raspberry Pi.

3. **Scalability Mechanisms**:
   - **No horizontal scaling** is mentioned; the system is designed for a single Raspberry Pi node.
   - The **future work section** (Document 2) does not specify scalability, but the current implementation is **not scalable** beyond the existing hardware.

4. **Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
   - The DT relies on the **Raspberry Pi’s hardware stability** and **RabbitMQ’s message persistence** for reliability.
   - No redundancy or failover mechanisms are explicitly documented, but the system is designed for robustness against minor sensor/control disruptions.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle stages for the Digital Twin (DT) of the incubator system, as implied by the provided context, are not explicitly detailed. However, the documents suggest that the DT lifecycle involves:
  - **Modeling Phase**: Creation of abstract representations (e.g., dynamic models described by differential equations) to understand system behavior, with models calibrated using data from the physical twin (PT) to refine cyber-physical system (CPS) and environmental parameters.
  - **Operational Phase**: Continuous integration of models, data, and algorithms (e.g., fault detectors, state estimators) into the DT for real-time monitoring, simulation, and predictive analysis of the PT’s performance under varying conditions (e.g., temperature regulation in the incubator).
  The documents do not provide explicit lifecycle governance frameworks or transition management protocols, as the focus is on the incubator case study’s hardware/software setup and experimental validation rather than lifecycle management details. The DT’s models are retained post-deployment for ongoing optimization and safety checks.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution for the incubator system, as described, involves:
  - **Modeling and Simulation**: Development of dynamic models (e.g., differential equations) to simulate the incubator’s behavior, calibrated using data from sensors (e.g., two internal and one external temperature sensors) to update model parameters.
  - **Calibration Algorithms**: Use of calibration methods to refine CPS and environmental models with real-time sensor data, ensuring accurate representation of the PT’s physical behavior (e.g., temperature regulation via a Raspberry Pi controller).
  - **Experimental Validation**: Conducted via the publicly available incubator case study repository ([link provided]), where experimental results demonstrate the DT’s ability to predict PT performance and detect violations (e.g., suboptimal temperature control).
  - **Evolution Management**: The DT’s evolution is implied by iterative calibration and model updates, though the documents do not specify formal methodologies (e.g., model-first approaches) or version control systems. The DT’s role is validated through empirical results (e.g., Section 4 of the report).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT’s decision support capabilities are rooted in **real-time data analytics**, **model-based predictions**, and **closed-loop control**, enabling insights for temperature regulation and system robustness. Key components include:

1. **Insights Generated**:
   - **Predictive Insights**: The DT generates predictions of the PT’s temperature behavior under current/forecasted conditions (e.g., external temperature variations) using calibrated thermal models. These predictions are derived from **differential equations** (e.g., Section 3 of Document 2) and validated via experimental results (e.g., Section 4).
   - **Anomaly Detection**: Monitors for deviations between PT data (e.g., sensor readings) and DT predictions, triggering alerts for **non-ideal conditions** (e.g., sensor drift, environmental changes). This is implemented via **supervisory controllers** (e.g., state estimators) that compare live data with model outputs.
   - **Optimization Insights**: Supports **real-time adjustments** to the PT’s control parameters (e.g., heatbed/fan settings) to achieve target temperatures (e.g., incubator’s desired temperature range). These adjustments are informed by **calibration algorithms** (e.g., parameter estimation) that optimize model accuracy.

2. **Analytics and Processing**:
   - **Data Processing**: Uses **sensor data streams** (e.g., temperature readings from 3 sensors: 2 inside the box, 1 outside) to update DT models in real-time. The **Raspberry Pi** acts as the control hub, processing data via **calibration algorithms** (e.g., parameter estimation) to adjust models dynamically.
   - **Model-Driven Analytics**: Relies on **high-fidelity thermal models** (e.g., differential equations) to simulate PT behavior under varying conditions (e.g., external temperature changes). These models are **calibrated iteratively** (e.g., Section 3) to improve accuracy.
   - **Confidence Measures**: Implicitly relies on **experimental validation** (e.g., Section 4) to assess the DT’s confidence in predictions. For example, the DT’s accuracy is evaluated by comparing predicted vs. actual temperature deviations during tests.

3. **Decision Making Processes**:
   - **Autonomous Decisions**: Algorithms (e.g., supervisory controllers) autonomously decide when to adjust PT parameters (e.g., heatbed/fan speed) based on DT predictions and PT data discrepancies. This is triggered by **monitoring violations** (e.g., temperature deviations exceeding thresholds).
   - **Human Interaction**: Humans (or engineers) can intervene in the decision-making process by overriding automated adjustments when necessary (e.g., Section 1 of Document 1). This is supported by the DT’s **visualization capabilities** (e.g., real-time temperature graphs) for decision support.
   - **Feedback Loops**: Decisions are iteratively refined via **experimental feedback** (e.g., Section 4), where DT performance is evaluated against PT outcomes. Future work (e.g., Section 5) suggests expanding this feedback loop to include **long-term operational data**.

**User Interaction Methods**:
   - **Visualization**: Implicitly supported by the DT’s **real-time monitoring** (e.g., temperature graphs) and **calibration feedback** (e.g., sensor data discrepancies).
   - **Alerts**: Provides alerts for critical deviations (e.g., temperature spikes) via the control system (e.g., Raspberry Pi).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin (DT) system integrates **calibration algorithms** to update models of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment with real-time sensor data for optimal parameter estimation. Safety is ensured through **monitoring systems** that continuously validate the system's operational adherence to predefined safety constraints (e.g., safe performance and fault detection). While the DT employs **state estimators** and **supervisory controllers** to detect deviations from expected behavior, the provided documents do not mention cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or fail-safe mechanisms beyond operational safety checks. The focus remains on **physical twin safety verification** rather than cybersecurity or data integrity protections.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container







Temperature







Controller


Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the Physical Twin as a CPS-based incubator for temperature regulation. It correctly lists the hardware components: styrofoam box ('A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container'), heatbed ('A heatbed to heat up the content'), fan ('A fan to distribute the heating'), two internal sensors ('Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside'), one external sensor ('One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside'), and the controller ('A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B was selected to implement the controller'). It also correctly characterizes the system's CPS nature and its suitability for digital twinning based on the text's description of the case study goals.

The extraction accurately captures the core services described in the introduction and modeling sections. It correctly identifies calibration using SciPy, safety monitoring, and the predictive purpose of the simulation models. The target users are reasonably inferred from the text's mention of an 'accessible platform' and 'open source' nature. Supported by quotes: 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models', 'monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally', 'run simulations that reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT'.

The extraction correctly lists the hardware (Raspberry Pi 4 Model B), software libraries (Python SciPy), communication protocols (RabbitMQ), and repository (GitHub). These are all explicitly mentioned: 'Raspberry Pi 4 Model B', 'optimisation function inside the python Scipy package', 'We used a Rabbit MQ server', and 'The publicly available repository [link]'. It also correctly identifies differential equations as the modeling framework ('dynamic model described by the differential equations') and lists enabling algorithms referenced in the DT definition.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately identifies the two simulation models (two-parameter and four-parameter) and correctly states the four-parameter model's superiority in predicting thermal inertia. It precisely details the data source (DS18S20 sensors, 0.5 °C accuracy) and transmission method (1-Wire bus). The description of model calibration using empirical data and the coupling between data and models is well-supported by the text. Quotes: 'one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters', 'latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia of the heatbed', 'DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer... accuracy of 0.5 °C'.

While the extraction correctly identifies the controller as a bang-bang type and the presence of a safety protocol, it contains factual errors regarding the fan. It states voltages are 'not explicitly quantified' despite the source text explicitly stating the fan 'can be run on 12V or 24V' and using 18V in experiments. It also misattributes 'thermally insulated' to the heatbed itself, whereas the text describes the styrofoam box as the insulated container.

The extraction accurately describes the sensor type (DS18S20), accuracy (0.5 °C), and transmission protocol (1-Wire bus). It correctly identifies the number of sensors (three) and their general purpose. It honestly states that sampling frequency is not explicitly quantified in the text, though the text mentions 'periodically reads'. The spatial distribution is summarized generally as 'strategically placed' which aligns with the text's description of placing sensors at specific hot/cold spots. Quotes: 'DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer... accuracy of 0.5 °C', 'allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus', 'three sensors were deployed'.

The extraction accurately captures the fidelity comparison between the 2-parameter and 4-parameter models, correctly noting the latter's superiority in predicting thermal inertia. It also accurately describes the validation method (experimental comparison with data trajectories) and the role of calibration in mitigating uncertainty and error. Supported by quotes: 'The latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia of the heatbed itself', 'resulting behaviour is compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by running an experiment', and 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
